caveats: a: The latest directly accessible detailed OEWS table is 2022 and covers NAICS 487100 rather than firm-size-specific LMM operators.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers, which can be material in owner-operated sightseeing firms.
a: Observed Claude usage is not equivalent to technically substitutable labor and is skewed toward digitally represented tasks.
rho: No industry-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was found.
rho: Current enterprise AI usage may not transfer to small seasonal operators with thin management capacity.
rho: Passenger-safety and operating duties remain human-accountable even when adjacent paperwork is automated.
e: The BLS private-versus-government establishment counts are for NAICS 487 as a whole, not 487110 or the LMM band.
e: Private status does not establish control transferability or fit with the recurring-service lens.
e: The industry mixes bus, trolley, rail, and animal-powered businesses with different capital and permit profiles.
s5: The Census owner-age statistic is all-industry, based on 2018 responding owners, and is not a firm-level probability.
s5: The EPI sample is voluntary, cross-industry, and likely unusually engaged in exit planning.
s5: An ownership transition is not necessarily an external, transferable control sale.
q: A single park-partner trolley is illustrative of posted ticket pricing, not an industry-wide contract sample.
q: Retention is likely lower for commoditized hop-on/hop-off routes and higher for scarce concession or rail experiences.
q: Seasonality and fixed departure capacity can convert labor savings into service expansion rather than cash margin.
d5: Real spending is not a direct measure of trip quantity or constant service quality.
d5: The forecast is national and broad; individual operators are highly exposed to local tourism, weather, wildfire, fuel, and concession conditions.
d5: The published forecast ends in 2030, slightly short of a full five years from the July 2026 run date.
o: Autonomous passenger vehicles are unlikely to diffuse uniformly within five years but could affect the high end of automation scenarios.
o: Narration requirements vary: live guides are central to some premium tours and peripheral to basic loop transportation.
o: Operator-required share could be lower where the destination itself, rather than transportation, creates most customer value.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily direct-to-consumer, prepaid per-passenger tickets or day passes for scheduled or reservable local experiences, with secondary group, private-hire, food, merchandise, and attraction-bundle revenue; repeat service is delivered across departures and seasons rather than through long-term customer contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Public or captive attraction transit, nonprofit museum operations, point-to-point passenger transportation, charter-only fleets, travel agencies, operators without transferable operating assets or permits, and businesses outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled U.S. operators of repeat, ticketed local land sightseeing experiences, including sightseeing buses and trolleys, scenic rail excursions, and horse-drawn sightseeing rides, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2022 employment and wage mix for NAICS 487100, while Anthropic measures observed Claude use by tasks and broad occupations rather than substitution in this industry. I mapped higher exposure to reservations, customer service, bookkeeping, marketing, scheduling, and parts of management and tour-content work, and low exposure to driving, vehicle/rail maintenance, cleaning, food service, and live safety duties, then wage-weighted those judgments using the BLS mix. This likely overstates substitution where administrative work is bundled into owner-manager roles and understates exposure where multilingual narration and itinerary content can be automated.
caveats: The latest directly accessible detailed OEWS table is 2022 and covers NAICS 487100 rather than firm-size-specific LMM operators.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers, which can be material in owner-operated sightseeing firms.
Observed Claude usage is not equivalent to technically substitutable labor and is skewed toward digitally represented tasks.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across 15-25 LMM sightseeing operators, pairing payroll by role with task logs for reservations, dispatch, customer support, marketing, narration preparation, driving, maintenance, and compliance, would materially tighten the wage-weighted exposure estimate.
a.rationale: The BLS mix is dominated by physical and guest-facing work: transportation and material moving is 32.88% of employment, personal care and service (principally tour and travel guides) is 25.00%, and installation/maintenance is 7.67%. The clearest digital pool is office and administrative support at 13.24%, plus portions of sales, marketing, accounting, scheduling, management, and scripted guide-content work. Anthropic reports routine back-office workflows such as email, document processing, CRM, and scheduling concentrating in automation-oriented API use, consistent with substantial exposure inside the administrative slice but not the vehicle operation itself.

d5.bridge: U.S. Travel forecasts inflation-adjusted national leisure-travel spending, not constant-quality trip quantities for land sightseeing. Its table rises from $1.043 trillion in 2025 to $1.178 trillion in 2030, a 1.13 ratio; I use approximately that real-spending path as the base quantity proxy for a five-year horizon. This may overstate quantity if premiumization or residual quality mix drives real spending and may understate destination-specific operators that gain share from shorter regional trips.
caveats: Real spending is not a direct measure of trip quantity or constant service quality.
The forecast is national and broad; individual operators are highly exposed to local tourism, weather, wildfire, fuel, and concession conditions.
The published forecast ends in 2030, slightly short of a full five years from the July 2026 run date.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain operator-level passenger counts and capacity by route for 2019-2026, then compare destination-specific five-year visitation forecasts, attraction attendance, hotel room nights, and advance bookings with constant-quality ticket-price indices.
d5.rationale: Land sightseeing is a local, same-day recreation product tied mainly to leisure visitation. The Spring 2026 U.S. Travel forecast shows continued real leisure-spending growth through 2030 and says travelers are shifting toward shorter, lower-cost regional and drive trips, which is broadly supportive of local land experiences, while consumer uncertainty, energy costs, weather, destination concentration, and discretionary-budget sensitivity justify a range spanning contraction to stronger growth.

caveats: The BLS private-versus-government establishment counts are for NAICS 487 as a whole, not 487110 or the LMM band.
Private status does not establish control transferability or fit with the recurring-service lens.
The industry mixes bus, trolley, rail, and animal-powered businesses with different capital and permit profiles.
e.change_evidence: Classify the frozen 24-firm LMM universe using state entity records, operator websites, nonprofit filings, concession/permit documents, ownership structure, and revenue mix; confirm transferability of permits, rolling stock, depot rights, and destination access with management interviews.
e.rationale: Most establishments in the broader scenic-transportation subsector are private, and the NAICS definition is inherently an external-customer sightseeing service. Eligibility is reduced for public or nonprofit operators, captive attraction transit, owner-dependent micro-operations, nontransferable permits or access rights, and firms whose economics are primarily adjacent retail, food, or real estate rather than repeat transportation experiences.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 487110 ($434,672,360) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 487110 ($1,260,637,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $150,362 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 487110); class <5: 411 firms, avg revenue/firm $418,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,167 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,111,478 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $109,258 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,884,697 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $185,266 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 74 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,144,514 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $505,706 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,178,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,590,322 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,534,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,215,133 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 24

caveats: Autonomous passenger vehicles are unlikely to diffuse uniformly within five years but could affect the high end of automation scenarios.
Narration requirements vary: live guides are central to some premium tours and peripheral to basic loop transportation.
Operator-required share could be lower where the destination itself, rather than transportation, creates most customer value.
o.change_evidence: Survey customers on willingness to substitute self-guided apps or autonomous experiences, measure guide-free versus live-guided conversion and satisfaction, and review operator staffing plus insurer/regulator requirements for each vehicle mode and jurisdiction.
o.rationale: The purchased service is the safe operation of a physical bus, trolley, train, or horse-drawn ride in a specific place, often with boarding, live guest handling, and scheduled departures. Software and self-guided audio can replace portions of planning, ticketing, and narration, but cannot supply the transportation equipment or discharge on-site operating responsibility; the residual displacement risk is mainly substitution toward self-guided walking/driving apps or fully prerecorded experiences.

caveats: A single park-partner trolley is illustrative of posted ticket pricing, not an industry-wide contract sample.
Retention is likely lower for commoditized hop-on/hop-off routes and higher for scarce concession or rail experiences.
Seasonality and fixed departure capacity can convert labor savings into service expansion rather than cash margin.
q.change_evidence: Analyze transaction-level ticket prices, channel commissions, promotion codes, group contracts, capacity utilization, and contribution margins for 3-5 years before and after workflow changes at a representative set of operators; pair this with local competitor fare histories.
q.rationale: Direct, posted per-passenger pricing gives operators more near-term retention than cost-plus or labor-hour contracts: a current NPS-hosted example lists fixed adult, concession, and child fares for scheduled 90-minute trolley departures. Benefits can nevertheless leak through discounting, OTA commissions, group negotiations, lower future price growth, added service expectations, and rapid imitation by competing operators at renewal or repurchase.

caveats: No industry-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was found.
Current enterprise AI usage may not transfer to small seasonal operators with thin management capacity.
Passenger-safety and operating duties remain human-accountable even when adjacent paperwork is automated.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain before-and-after staffing, service-level, error-rate, and software-adoption data from reservation platforms such as FareHarbor or Peek Pro and interview operators that have deployed AI support, dispatch, pricing, and marketing tools for at least one full peak season.
rho.rationale: Within five years, established reservation, support, marketing, scheduling, and document workflows should permit meaningful implementation, but seasonal staffing, fragmented systems, small IT budgets, exception-heavy guest service, safety accountability, and owner-operator dependence will slow conversion of technical exposure into avoided hiring. The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not to the physical majority of the workforce.

s5.bridge: The Exit Planning Institute surveys owners across more than 25 industries and reports intention to exit, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM scenic-transport firms. I start from the reported 49% intending to exit within five years and discount heavily for self-selection, internal family or management transitions, delayed or failed sales, asset closures, and firms that cannot transfer operating rights. The proxy could overstate qualifying transfers because intent exceeds completion, but it could understate forced or opportunistic sales not planned at the survey date.
caveats: The Census owner-age statistic is all-industry, based on 2018 responding owners, and is not a firm-level probability.
The EPI sample is voluntary, cross-industry, and likely unusually engaged in exit planning.
An ownership transition is not necessarily an external, transferable control sale.
s5.change_evidence: Compile five years of closed 487110 control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, BizBuySell, Axial, broker tombstones, and state concession-transfer records, divided by the eligible LMM firm population; interview tourism-business brokers about failed and internal successions.
s5.rationale: Owner demographics and stated exit intentions support a meaningful transaction pool: Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least 55 in 2018, while EPI reports 49% of its 2023 owner respondents wanted to exit within five years. EPI also finds strong preference for internal exits among family-company respondents, so only a minority of the intent pool is treated as a qualifying external control transfer.

principal_driver: Direct ticket economics around a repeat physical experience allow back-office AI savings to remain with the operator more readily than in pass-through service contracts.
principal_weakness: Most wages sit in physical, safety-sensitive, and guest-facing work, while the eligible transaction universe is small and transferability can depend on local permits and destination access.
